_meta.title: The Actor Model — Erlang and Akka principles

questions.question: What fundamental property distinguishes the actor model from shared-memory concurrency?
options: Actors use locks to protect critical sections
Actors communicate exclusively through asynchronous message passing and never share memory
Actors must run on separate physical cores to be considered concurrent
Actors share a global mailbox that all actors read from
questions.explanation: The actor model forbids shared memory entirely. Each actor owns its state, and communication happens only through asynchronous messages sent to a per-actor mailbox. No locks are needed because there is no shared state to protect.

questions.question: In Erlang/OTP, what does the 'let it crash' philosophy mean in practice?
options: Developers should write code that never raises exceptions
If a process encounters an error, it should catch it and continue silently
Processes should fail fast on unexpected errors; supervisors detect the crash and restart the process to a known good state
The entire system should crash immediately when any single process dies
questions.explanation: 'Let it crash' means you avoid defensive error-handling code. Instead, you let the process fail and rely on a supervisor to detect the failure (via linked processes or monitors) and restart the process to a clean initial state. This produces simpler, more robust systems.

questions.question: How does the Erlang counter actor maintain state across messages?
options: It writes state to a global ETS table after each message
It stores state in a process dictionary
It passes the state as a parameter in a tail-recursive function call
It writes state to disk after every message
questions.explanation: An Erlang actor's state is a function parameter passed via tail recursion. For example, `counter(Count) -> receive {increment, N} -> counter(Count + N) end.` The actor loops by tail-calling itself with the updated count. This keeps the state entirely encapsulated within the actor's own call stack.

questions.question: What does 'location transparency' mean in the context of actor systems like Erlang and Akka?
options: Actors can only run on the local machine for security reasons
The address of an actor (pid or ActorRef) works the same whether the actor is on the local node or a remote node
Actors are automatically migrated to the nearest geographical location
Actors must know the physical IP address of every other actor they communicate with
questions.explanation: Location transparency means that sending a message to an actor looks the same regardless of where that actor lives — on the same node or across a network. Erlang's `Pid ! Msg` syntax works across nodes transparently. Akka achieves this with `ActorRef`, which resolves to a local or remote actor behind the same API.

questions.question: How does the Actor model handle concurrency?
options: By sharing a global lock across all actors
Each actor has private state and communicates only by sending immutable messages to other actors' mailboxes
By using shared memory with fine-grained locking
By executing all actors on a single thread
questions.explanation: In the Actor model (Hewitt, 1973), actors are the universal primitives: each actor has a mailbox, private state, and behavior. They communicate exclusively via asynchronous messages. No shared state means no locks, no data races. Erlang/OTP and Akka implement this model at scale.
